questions.q: The $j$-invariant is the unique $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$-invariant holomorphic function on $\mathcal{H}$ whose Fourier expansion begins:
questions.explain: $j(\tau) = q^{-1} + 744 + 196884\,q + 21493760\,q^2 + \cdots$ with $q = e^{2\pi i\tau}$. The pole is at the cusp $q=0$; the constant term $744$ is a normalization; the higher coefficients are the McKay–Thompson data.

questions.q: What is the coefficient of $q$ (i.e., $c_1$) in the Fourier expansion of $j(\tau)$?
questions.explain: $c_1 = 196884$. Numerically one unit more than the dimension $196883$ of the smallest non-trivial irreducible representation of the Monster — McKay's starting observation.

choices: a different pole at the cusp
vanishing constant term and otherwise the same coefficients
questions.explain: Subtracting $744$ only changes the constant term; the pole $q^{-1}$ and every $c_n$ for $n \ge 1$ are unchanged. $J$ is the version whose graded-dimension statement, $J = \sum_{n} (\dim V^\natural_n) q^n$, is cleanest.

monster-group-structure.title: The Monster group and its irreducibles

choices: the largest sporadic finite simple group
questions.explain: $\mathbb{M}$ is the largest of the $26$ sporadic finite simple groups, of order $\approx 8\times 10^{53}$. The other finite simple groups sit in infinite families (cyclic of prime order, alternating, Lie type); $\mathbb{M}$ does not.

questions.q: What is the dimension of the smallest faithful (i.e., non-trivial) complex irreducible representation of the Monster?
questions.explain: $\dim V_2 = 196883$. The Griess algebra on which $\mathbb{M}$ was originally constructed is $196883$-dimensional (its identity element adds one more, giving the $196884$-dimensional 'Griess algebra plus identity' often quoted).

These $15$ primes — the 'supersingular primes' in the sense of Ogg — are exactly the primes $p$ for which $\Gamma_0(p)+$ has genus $0$. Ogg conjectured this coincidence before moonshine existed, and Conway–Norton's conjecture explained it.

$196884 = 196883 + 1 = \dim V_2 + \dim V_1$. McKay's original line: the $q$-coefficient of $j$ equals the sum of the two smallest irreducible dimensions of the Monster — numerically trivial, mathematically a miracle.

questions.q: Compute $21296876 + 196883 + 1$ and compare to the $q^2$-coefficient of $j(\tau)$.
questions.explain: $21493760 = 21296876 + 196883 + 1 = \dim V_3 + \dim V_2 + \dim V_1 = c_2$. McKay's list continues: every $c_n$ decomposes as a non-negative integer combination of Monster irreducible dimensions.

questions.q: Under McKay's conjectural interpretation, the $q^n$-coefficient $c_n$ of $j(\tau)$ equals the graded dimension of:
choices: the $n$-th symmetric power of a Monster representation
the weight-$n$ piece $V^\natural_n$ of a graded Monster module $V^\natural$
a direct summand of the regular representation of $\mathbb{M}$
questions.explain:
The conjecture: there is a graded $\mathbb{M}$-module $V^
atural =  igoplus_n V^
atural_n$ with $\dim V^
atural_n = c_n$. Its graded character (replacing dimension by $\mathrm{tr}\,g$) gives Thompson series; Frenkel–Lepowsky–Meurman built $V^
atural$ explicitly as a vertex operator algebra.

questions.q: The Conway–Norton conjecture states that for each conjugacy class $[g]$ of $\mathbb{M}$, the Thompson series $T_g(\tau)$ is:
choices: a Hecke eigenform of weight $0$ for $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$
the Hauptmodul of a genus-zero discrete subgroup of $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{R})$
questions.explain: Hauptmodul = generator of the function field of a genus-zero modular curve $\Gamma_g\backslash\mathcal{H}^*$, normalized by its pole at the cusp. The full list of possible $\Gamma_g$ is a specific set of $171$ commensurable-with-$\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$ groups.

questions.q: For the identity element $e \in \mathbb{M}$, the Thompson series $T_e(\tau)$ equals:
questions.explain:
$\mathrm{tr}(e | V^
atural_n) = \dim V^
atural_n = c_n$, the $q^n$-coefficient of $J = j - 744$. The constant term vanishes because $V^
atural_0 = 0$ (the 'vacuum' lives in degree $-1$).

questions.q: For the class 2A in $\mathbb{M}$, the character values are $\chi_1(g) = 1$ and $\chi_2(g) = 4371$. Compute the coefficient of $q$ in $T_{2A}(\tau)$.
questions.explain: $V^\natural_1 = V_1 \oplus V_2$ (McKay's decomposition), so $\mathrm{tr}(g | V^\natural_1) = \chi_1(g) + \chi_2(g) = 1 + 4371 = 4372$. This is the $q$-coefficient of the Hauptmodul of $\Gamma_0(2)+$.

borcherds-proof-sketch.title: Borcherds' proof — generalized Kac–Moody and replication

choices: a generalized Kac–Moody algebra (the Monster Lie algebra) with root multiplicities $c(mn)$
a new infinite family of sporadic simple groups
a modular form of weight $\tfrac{1}{2}$ attached to each Thompson series
questions.explain: The Monster Lie algebra $\mathfrak{m}$ is a generalized Kac–Moody algebra whose root $(m,n)$ (for $m > 0, n \in \mathbb{Z}$) has multiplicity $c(mn)$, the $j$-coefficient at $mn$. This is the object whose existence is forced by the Goddard–Thorn no-ghost theorem applied to $V^\natural$ plus lattice bosons.

questions.q: The Weyl–Kac denominator identity for the Monster Lie algebra reads:
questions.explain: This infinite product, viewed as a formal power series in $p$ and $q$, equals $J(p) - J(q)$. Equating coefficients of $p^m q^n$ produces 'replication' recursions; each Thompson series satisfies the same recursion with character values in place of dimensions, forcing it to be a Hauptmodul.

questions.q: The Goddard–Thorn no-ghost theorem enters the proof by:
choices: proving the Riemann hypothesis for $L$-functions of Thompson series
identifying the physical state space of a bosonic string with a Lie algebra whose root-space dimensions match $c(mn)$
constructing the Monster as a Galois group
questions.explain: Goddard–Thorn (1972, from string theory) identifies the BRST-cohomological physical states of a bosonic string on a certain background with a Lie algebra carrying an explicit root decomposition. Applied to $V^\natural \otimes V_{1,1}$ (Leech-lattice-like bosons), the output is the Monster Lie algebra.
